title: Long-Range Order in Coupled $D$-dimensional Kuramoto Oscillators
authors: Zhongpu Qiu, Tianyi Wu, Linkai Zhang, Sheng Fang, Jun Meng, Jingfang Fan, Hugues Chaté
abstract: We show that the long-range order (LRO) strikingly emerges in systems of locally coupled $D$-dimensional vector Kuramoto oscillators on low-dimensional lattices ($d=1,2$), but only for odd $D$. This parity-dependent effect is traced to two-oscillator dynamics, where odd-$D$ units synchronize for any coupling, while even-$D$ pairs require a finite threshold. This fundamental difference selectively seeds collective order in large-scale systems, a phenomenon demonstrated by our numerical simulations. A renormalization group analysis reveals a RG flow to a weak-coupling fixed point for $d \le 2$. In this limit, odd-$D$ systems effectively map to a ferromagnetic model, developing an ordered ``hemisphere" phase, whereas even-$D$ systems remain disordered. Our findings further reveal orientational LRO emerges in both $d=1$ and $d=2$, but frequency LRO requires $d=2$. We contrast these results with the established behavior of models possessing continuous symmetry, highlighting how quenched disorder provides a fundamentally new route to order.
summary: The paper explores the emergence of long-range order in $D$-dimensional Kuramoto oscillators on 1D and 2D lattices. It discovers a parity effect where order only appears when the oscillator dimension $D$ is odd. This happens because odd-$D$ systems possess a null space in their frequency matrices that allows for synchronization, effectively mapping the system to a ferromagnetic model. In contrast, even-$D$ systems remain disordered. The study highlights how quenched noise can serve as a stabilizing force against the fluctuations that usually prevent order in low dimensions.
main_problem: Investigating whether quenched frequency disorder can stabilize long-range order (LRO) in low-dimensional (d=1, 2) vector Kuramoto oscillators, thereby circumventing the Hohenberg-Mermin-Wagner theorem.
main_result: Long-range order emerges in low-dimensional lattices only for odd-dimensional oscillators (D), where quenched noise acts as a stabilizing mechanism that maps the system to an effective ferromagnetic model.
method: The authors use two-body dynamics analysis, large-scale numerical simulations, and a real-space renormalization group (RG) framework combined with Bogoliubov-Krylov averaging.
model_or_system: A D-dimensional vector Kuramoto model (DDKM) on 1D and 2D lattices, featuring alignment coupling and quenched noise introduced via antisymmetric intrinsic frequency matrices.
key_observables: Orientational order parameter (rho) and frequency order parameter (r, measuring the fraction of entrained oscillators).
important_parameters: Oscillator dimension (D), lattice dimension (d), coupling strength (K), and the presence of quenched frequency disorder.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the use of antisymmetric matrices for frequency disorder and focuses on the thermodynamic limit via RG.
figures_summary: Figure 4 shows the decay of order parameters (rho and r) versus coupling strength (K) for even-D oscillators, demonstrating the absence of LRO.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the model and the problem of LRO in low dimensions, analyzes microscopic two-body dynamics, presents large-scale numerical simulations, performs a real-space renormalization group analysis, and concludes by comparing the results to standard continuous symmetry models.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to many-body dynamics as it demonstrates a novel mechanism where quenched disorder, typically a source of decoherence or disorder, actually stabilizes long-range order in low-dimensional systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the D-dimensional Kuramoto model. Each oscillator ⃗σi evolves under (i) intrinsic rotation Wi⃗σi (generalizing natural frequencies in classical Kuramoto mod- els) and (ii) coupling hij to neighbors ⃗σj ∈Λi. The coupling term hij = K[⃗b −(⃗a ·⃗b)⃗a] aligns ⃗σi with the orthogonal com- ponent of ⃗σj, promoting synchronization (generalizing sine coupling).

FIG. 2. Phase diagrams for (a) d = 1, (b) d = 2, and (c) d = 3 dimensional coupled D = 3 oscillators with varying system size L. Log-log-log plots of the orientational order parameter ρ versus the inverse system size L−1 at different coupling strengths K for (d) d = 1, (e) d = 2, and (f) d = 3 systems, where colors indicate the values of the order param- eter.

FIG. 3. Spatial patterns and frequency entrainment for cou- pled D = 3 oscillators. (a) d = 1: Finite entrained clusters with functional network structure inserted. (b) d = 2 (c) d = 3: The largest cluster projected on the D = 3 sphere exhibiting hemisphere phase. (d)-(f) Frequency order param- eter r versus coupling strength K for different system sizes L in d = 1, 2, 3 dimensions respectively, where panel (d) uses log scale for the vertical axis.

FIG. 4. Order parameters vs coupling strength K for even-D oscillators in d = 2 dimensions. (a,c,e) Orientational order parameter ρ and (b,d,f) frequency order parameter r for D = 4, 6, 8 respectively, with different system length L.
